content.text: A fault tolerant system continues operating correctly even when one or more of its components fail. Unlike high availability — which tolerates brief downtime during failover — a truly fault tolerant system has zero observable disruption to users. This is achieved by running redundant active components simultaneously, so the failure of any single component is absorbed without a failover event.

attrs.alt: Comparison of fault tolerance, high availability, and disaster recovery patterns: trade-offs in downtime, cost, and architecture

content.text: RTO and RPO: The Two Fundamental Metrics
Before choosing a resilience strategy, define two objectives that quantify your tolerance for failure:
Maximum acceptable downtime after a failure event
Architecture: active-active vs failover vs restore
Maximum acceptable data loss measured in time
Replication mode: synchronous vs async vs backup

RTO and RPO are business decisions, not engineering decisions. Engineering implements them — product and legal set the targets. A payment processing system might need RTO=0, RPO=0. An internal reporting dashboard might accept RTO=4 hours, RPO=24 hours.

The relationship between RTO/RPO and cost is roughly exponential: cutting RTO from 1 hour to zero can increase infrastructure cost by 3–5×. Always get explicit stakeholder sign-off before committing to strict targets.
Fault tolerance has a precise meaning: the system continues without any degradation of service when N components fail, where N is the designed fault tolerance level. This requires:
— every component that can fail must have at least one identical copy handling live traffic simultaneously.
— no component whose failure would interrupt the service. Every layer must be redundant: compute, storage, network, DNS.
— a health check or heartbeat detects the failure within milliseconds, not minutes.
— in-flight requests are either completed or transparently retried by the redundant component, with no client-visible error.

True zero-downtime fault tolerance is expensive because you pay for capacity that handles production traffic while also providing a hot spare. The decision is almost always economic: is the cost of downtime greater than the cost of full redundancy?
AWS Services Designed for Fault Tolerance
Several AWS services implement fault tolerance natively — you get it automatically without additional architecture:
Data stored across ≥3 AZs; automatic reconstruction on disk failure
Multi-AZ replication within a region; synchronous writes to 3 AZs
Messages stored redundantly across multiple servers and AZs
Runs in multiple AZs; retries failed invocations (async)
Nodes run across multiple AZs; auto-scales, replaces unhealthy nodes
Anycast routing; DNS queries answered from multiple PoPs globally

These services require you to architect for fault tolerance at the application level — for example, designing S3 object access patterns to handle eventual consistency in scenarios like versioned writes.

EC2 instances are not inherently fault tolerant — a single instance is a single point of failure. Building fault tolerant compute on EC2 requires combining Auto Scaling and load balancing across multiple AZs:

# Auto Scaling Group across 3 AZs with ALB
resource "aws_autoscaling_group" "web" {
  name                = "web-asg"
  min_size            = 3   # One per AZ minimum
  max_size            = 12
  desired_capacity    = 3
  vpc_zone_identifier = [
    aws_subnet.az_a.id,
    aws_subnet.az_b.id,
    aws_subnet.az_c.id,
  ]
  target_group_arns = [aws_lb_target_group.web.arn]

  health_check_type         = "ELB"   # Not EC2 — use ALB health checks
  health_check_grace_period = 60

  # Replace unhealthy instances automatically
  lifecycle {
    create_before_destroy = true
  }
}
With this configuration, if one AZ fails entirely, the ASG's
ensures replacement instances are launched in the remaining AZs. The ALB stops routing to the failed AZ's targets automatically within ~30 seconds (2 failed health checks × 15-second interval).
RDS Multi-AZ: High Availability, Not Fault Tolerance
RDS Multi-AZ is frequently misunderstood. It is high availability, not fault tolerance. There is a distinction:
maintains a synchronous standby replica in a second AZ. On primary failure, RDS promotes the standby automatically. The DNS endpoint is updated. Total downtime: 60–120 seconds.
is an asynchronous copy — it can lag behind the primary by seconds or minutes. Read replicas improve read throughput but do not reduce RPO for the primary write path.

is closer to fault tolerant: the cluster volume is replicated synchronously across 3 AZs (6 copies). A writer failure promotes a reader in under 30 seconds. Individual disk failures are healed automatically.

# RDS Multi-AZ for high availability
resource "aws_db_instance" "postgres" {
  identifier        = "prod-postgres"
  engine            = "postgres"
  instance_class    = "db.m6g.large"
  allocated_storage = 100
  multi_az          = true   # Synchronous standby in second AZ
  # RTO ~60-120s on AZ failure
  # RPO = 0 (synchronous replication)
}

# Aurora is closer to fault tolerant
resource "aws_rds_cluster" "aurora" {
  cluster_identifier = "prod-aurora"
  engine             = "aurora-postgresql"
  availability_zones = ["us-east-1a", "us-east-1b", "us-east-1c"]
  # 6-way replication; writer failure → reader promoted in <30s
}
The fundamental fault tolerance architecture choice is active-active versus active-passive:
All nodes (N nodes, N handling requests)
Higher — you pay for N nodes at full capacity
Lower — standby can be smaller or off
Low — standby is waste until needed
Harder — writes go to multiple nodes
ALB + ASG, DynamoDB Global Tables, Route 53 weighted routing

Choose active-active when your application can tolerate the consistency complexity (stateless services, eventually consistent datastores) and when the cost of downtime justifies paying for full redundancy. Choose active-passive when data consistency is paramount and brief failover downtime is acceptable.
Identifying and Eliminating Single Points of Failure
A structured approach to fault tolerance starts with a Single Point of Failure (SPOF) analysis. Walk the entire request path and mark every component that has no redundant counterpart:
— are you using Route 53 (distributed, 100% SLA) or a self-managed DNS server? A single DNS server is a SPOF.
— is it ELB/ALB (multi-AZ, managed) or a single EC2 instance running nginx? A single load balancer is a SPOF.
— single EC2 instance is a SPOF. Replace with ASG minimum size ≥ 2 across ≥ 2 AZs.
— single RDS instance is a SPOF. Enable Multi-AZ. For Aurora, multi-AZ is intrinsic.
— single ElastiCache node is a SPOF. Use replication groups (Redis) or multiple nodes (Memcached cluster mode).
— SQS is inherently redundant. A self-managed RabbitMQ on a single EC2 is a SPOF.
— EBS is replicated within a single AZ but not across AZs. A database on EBS fails with the AZ. Use EFS (multi-AZ) or RDS (Multi-AZ) for cross-AZ durability.
— a single NAT Gateway in one AZ creates an AZ dependency for all private subnets. Place one NAT Gateway per AZ.
Automatic failure detection is a prerequisite for fault tolerance. AWS provides layered health checks:
— AWS hardware-level checks. Instance status check + system status check. Failure triggers CloudWatch alarm.
— HTTP probe to a specific path (e.g., /health). Unhealthy targets are removed from rotation within 2 × health check interval.
— can check endpoints globally and route away from a failed region or AZ. Supports HTTP, HTTPS, TCP, and calculated (composite) checks.
— detect when an instance fails ELB health check and automatically terminate and replace it.

# ALB health check configuration
resource "aws_lb_target_group" "web" {
  name        = "web-tg"
  port        = 80
  protocol    = "HTTP"
  vpc_id      = aws_vpc.main.id

  health_check {
    path                = "/health"
    interval            = 15       # Check every 15 seconds
    timeout             = 5
    healthy_threshold   = 2        # 2 successes → healthy
    unhealthy_threshold = 2        # 2 failures → unhealthy (30s total)
    matcher             = "200"
  }
}
Fault Tolerance at the Application Layer
Infrastructure fault tolerance is necessary but not sufficient. The application layer must also be designed for resilience:
— operations must be safe to retry. A payment deducted twice is worse than a payment timing out. Use idempotency keys on all state-mutating requests.
— stop calling a downstream service that is failing. Fail fast with a cached response or error rather than queueing requests that will time out.
— all network calls must have explicit timeouts. Retry with exponential backoff and jitter to avoid thundering herd during recovery.
— if a non-critical feature (recommendations, search, analytics) fails, the core user flow should continue working.
— separate thread pools or connection pools for different downstream services. A slow database should not exhaust all connections and starve unrelated API calls.
A fault tolerance design that has not been tested is a hypothesis, not a guarantee. AWS provides tools for controlled failure injection:
— managed chaos engineering service. Define experiments that inject EC2 failures, network latency, CPU stress, RDS failovers, and more. Measure the system's actual recovery behavior.
— manually terminate instances in production or staging to verify ASG replaces them and ALB health checks remove them within the expected window.
— route all traffic away from one AZ by modifying route tables or removing subnets from the ALB. Observe whether the system continues serving requests.
— scheduled exercises where a team deliberately induces failures to test response procedures, monitoring alerts, and recovery playbooks.

These are the four DR/fault-tolerance strategies from the AWS Well-Architected Framework. Most organizations use a mix: active-active for their core user-facing services, warm standby for supporting services, and backup-restore for data archives and non-production environments.

AZ redundancy protects against data center failures. For regional failures — natural disasters, prolonged AWS service outages, or infrastructure events affecting an entire region — you need multi-region architecture:
— direct users to the nearest healthy region. Route 53 health checks detect regional failures and redirect traffic within the DNS TTL.
— multi-master, multi-region replication with single-digit millisecond latency globally. Writes accepted in any region; changes propagate to all others within seconds.
— one primary region handles writes; up to 5 secondary regions receive asynchronous replication with < 1 second lag. Failover to a secondary promotes it to primary in < 1 minute.
— asynchronous object-level replication between buckets in different regions. Not fault tolerant (async means some lag) but provides fast recovery.
Fault tolerance is much easier to achieve for stateless components than stateful ones:
ASG + ALB across AZs; any instance can handle any request
Redis cluster mode with replicas across AZs; Memcached cluster
Aurora Multi-AZ; failover has brief interruption (< 30 s)
SQS (inherently redundant); Kafka multi-AZ MSK cluster
Low for S3; Medium for EFS (mount targets per AZ)

The general principle: externalize all state into managed services (S3, DynamoDB, ElastiCache, RDS) and keep your application servers completely stateless. A stateless server can be terminated and replaced without affecting any in-flight request that has been passed to a managed service.
ElastiCache Redis for Fault Tolerant Caching
A single Redis node is a SPOF for any application that depends on it. ElastiCache Redis replication groups provide fault tolerance:
— one primary node + one or more read replicas across AZs. On primary failure, ElastiCache promotes a replica automatically in about 60 seconds.
— data sharded across up to 500 nodes. Each shard has a primary and replicas. Shard failure → replica promotion. True horizontal fault tolerance.
— cross-region replication for disaster recovery. One primary region; up to two secondary regions can promote to primary.

resource "aws_elasticache_replication_group" "cache" {
  replication_group_id = "prod-cache"
  description          = "Fault tolerant Redis cluster"
  node_type            = "cache.r6g.large"
  num_cache_clusters   = 3       # 1 primary + 2 replicas across AZs
  automatic_failover_enabled = true  # Auto-promotes replica on primary failure
  multi_az_enabled     = true
  subnet_group_name    = aws_elasticache_subnet_group.cache.name
  security_group_ids   = [aws_security_group.cache.id]
}
Fault tolerance only works if failures are actually detected and recovered. Monitor the key signals:
. Alert when the gap exceeds 1 (not enough healthy instances).
in any target group warrants investigation, even if traffic is still flowing.
— subscribe to RDS event categories 'failover' and 'notification' via SNS. A Multi-AZ failover is expected behavior but worth alerting on to measure actual RTO.
: 0 means the health check is failing. Alert immediately.
indicate when DynamoDB cannot serve requests, even though it is fault tolerant at the storage level.

# CloudWatch alarm: ALB has unhealthy targets
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "unhealthy_hosts" {
  alarm_name          = "alb-unhealthy-hosts"
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanThreshold"
  evaluation_periods  = 1
  metric_name         = "UnHealthyHostCount"
  namespace           = "AWS/ApplicationELB"
  period              = 60
  statistic           = "Maximum"
  threshold           = 0
  dimensions = {
    LoadBalancer = aws_lb.web.arn_suffix
    TargetGroup  = aws_lb_target_group.web.arn_suffix
  }
  alarm_actions = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]
}
— the most common mistake. Always use at least two AZs for any production workload, three for critical systems.
One NAT Gateway for all subnets
— all private subnets in all AZs route internet traffic through a single NAT Gateway in one AZ. If that AZ fails, all private instances lose internet access. Use one NAT Gateway per AZ.
— EBS volumes are scoped to a single AZ. Putting a database on EBS without Multi-AZ means AZ failure = data unavailable. Use RDS Multi-AZ or Aurora.

— a health check that returns 200 even when the application is broken defeats the entire fault tolerance mechanism. Health checks must verify actual dependencies (database connection, cache reachability).
— storing user sessions on EC2 instance memory means a failed instance loses all active sessions. Store sessions in ElastiCache or DynamoDB.
— a health check interval of 300 seconds means a failed instance serves 5 minutes of bad traffic before being removed. Keep intervals at 10–30 seconds for production traffic.
Fault tolerance means zero observable downtime during a component failure, achieved by running redundant active components — not by failover.
RTO (how long can you be down?) and RPO (how much data can you lose?) are business requirements that determine which architecture to build.
Eliminate every single point of failure systematically: NAT Gateways, load balancers, compute, databases, caches, and DNS must all have redundant counterparts across multiple AZs.
Many AWS services (S3, DynamoDB, Route 53, ELB) are fault tolerant by design — use them as building blocks rather than managing your own redundancy.
Test fault tolerance through AWS FIS chaos experiments, scheduled GameDays, and AZ-level failure simulations — an untested design is a hypothesis.
